items.q: What happens to the total resistance when you add another resistor in parallel?
items.correct: Total resistance decreases — there are more paths for current to flow.
items.misconception: Adding any resistor always increases the total resistance.
items.random_wrong: Adding a parallel resistor doubles the voltage available.
items.why: Students apply the series rule (resistances add) to parallel circuits because they don't visualise multiple paths reducing the overall opposition.

items.q: How do you see a non-luminous object like a book?
items.correct: Light from a source reflects off the book and enters your eyes.
items.misconception: Your eyes send out beams of vision that hit the book and let you see it.
items.random_wrong: The book emits a special invisible signal that your brain detects directly.
items.why: The 'extramission' model of vision is a stubborn childhood intuition, reinforced by phrases like 'cast your eye over' or comic-book vision rays.

items.q: Can a device ever be 100 percent efficient?
items.correct: In practice, no — some energy is always transferred to less useful stores (e.g. heat to surroundings).
items.misconception: A well-designed device can be 100 percent efficient by eliminating all friction.
items.random_wrong: Efficiency above 100 percent is possible if the device extracts ambient energy.
items.why: Students think 'inefficiency' is a design flaw to be removed, missing that the second law of thermodynamics ensures some dissipation.

items.q: A book on a high shelf has lots of energy in its:
items.why: It's gravitational store. “kinetic store” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Petrol stores energy in which form?
items.why: Correct answer: chemical. “thermal” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many joules of work are done when a 10 N force moves an object 3 m?
items.why: Exact answer: 30 J. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
